headline: Imported Toxic Waste Containers Found at 4 Malaysian Ports
context: Malaysian Customs detected 284 containers of mislabelled toxic waste at Port Klang, Penang Port, Tanjung Pelepas, and Kuantan Port in 2025, a 45% increase from 2023.

cards.big: 284 containers of toxic waste entered Malaysia mislabelled as recyclables.
cards.sub: 45% more than 2023. Malaysia remains a dumping ground for waste that developed nations will not process.

cards.big: Only 12% of incoming waste containers are physically inspected.
cards.sub: Scanner capacity covers 40%. The remaining 48% pass on documentation alone.

cards.big: Returning illegal waste costs Malaysia RM85,000 per container.
cards.sub: Total return cost for 284 containers: RM24.1M — paid by Malaysian taxpayers, not the exporters.

cards.big: The Basel Convention prohibits hazardous waste transfers to non-consenting countries.
cards.sub: Enforcement relies on importing countries detecting the waste. The system fails when inspection is 12%.

cards.big: International waste law says this should not happen. 284 containers say it does.
Malaysia should require waste exporters to post bonds covering return shipping costs. Shifting the financial risk from taxpayers to exporters would deter mislabelling overnight.
